Marine aquariums and their impact

wildsingapore homepage

wild shores of singapore blog

all animals | all plants | concepts | glossary | search

index of concepts **Marine aquariums**updated May 08

if you learn only 3 things about them …
 

Marine aquariums are a popular hobby. Irresponsible practice of this hobby, however, damages the shores and reefs.

Some facts (taken from Wabnitz, Colette, Michelle Taylor, Edmund Green and Tries Razak, From Ocean to Aquarium: The global trade in marine ornamental species, UNEP World Conservation Monitoring Centre, 2003)

If done right, some feel the aquarium trade can provide strong economic incentives for coral reef conservation.

  • Most marine life in the aquarium trade are NOT used for other purposes.
  • Aquarium animals are the highest value-added product that can be harvested from a coral reef.
  • If managed sustainably, the trade could provide jobs to low-income communities where there are few alternatives sources of income.

The aquarium trade is big

  • 1.5-2 million people worldwide are believed to engage in this hobby.
  • The trade supporting the hobby is worth an estimated US$200-300 million a year.

Where do the stock come from? Where does it go?

  • Unlike freshwater aquaria where 90% of traded stock are farmed, most of marine aquarium stock are wild caught. Only 1-10% of marine ornamental fish are captive-bred and less than 1% of hard corals are from cultured origins.
  • Southeast Asia is the source of animals for most of this trade.
  • The main destination is the US, Europe and to a lesser extent, Japan.

What is traded?

  • About 1,400 species of fishes are traded worldwide, or about 20-24 million individual fishes annually.
    • The 10 most traded species make up 36% of the trade.
    • Two species known NOT to do well in captivity nevertheless are in the top ten most traded species: these are the Cleaner wrasse (Labroides dimidiatus) and the mandarin fish (Synchiropus splendidus).
    • Damselfish (Family Pomacentridae) make up almost half of the trade.
  • About 140 species of hard corals are traded worldwide, or about 11-12 million pieces annually.
    • Species in 7 genera make up more than half of the live coral trade.
  • About 61 species of soft corals are traded worldwide, or about 390,000 pieces annually.
    • Dendronephthya is one of the most commonly traded species but most usually die within weeks due to difficulty in replicating natural conditions in the aquarium.
  • More than 500 species of other marine creatures are traded worldwide, or about 9-10 million animals annually. Molluscs, shrimps and anemones make up most of these.
    • Cleaner shrimps and the large sea anemones (Heteractis sp.) account for 15% of invertebrates traded.
  • 1-10% of fishes and less than 1% of corals are captive bred for sale. Thus almost all marine creatures sold in the aquarium trade are harvested from the wild.

Collection methods damage the reefs:

  • Live fish for the aquarium trade are often collected using cyanide or toxins. These kills off other reef inhabitants that cannot swim away. And the fishes collected in this manner often do not survive long.
  • Some marine invertebrates are collected by destroying the dead or living corals where they hide.
  • Overharvesting destroys a local population and can endanger the species.
  • A large proportion of the animals collected die during shipping and processing.

Many marine creatures cannot survive in captivity. Here are some of the reasons…

  • Require special diets that are difficult to duplicate in captivity. Many such creatures simply die of starvation in a marine aquarium
  • Require special habitats that are difficult to simulate. Such creatures die of stress in a marine aquarium.
  • Dangerous to fish-keeper or other tank mates. These creatures may kill off everything else in a marine aquarium.
  • Grow too large to be kept in a home aquarium. These creatures eventually have to be killed when they grow up.

Poaching on our shores and reefs for home or commercial aquariums continues to take place. Here is a documented attempt to remove hard corals from Labrador Beach. Such activities can seriously affect local populations of marine life.

Sustainable harvesting of reefs for the marine aquarium trade?

Some conservationists suggest that if managed properly, the aquarium trade could support long-term conservation and sustainable use of reefs.

  • Harvesting for the aquarium trade results in more monetary returns for smaller damage. 1 kg of aquarium fish is worth US$500, but as food, only $6 (Maldives, in 2000). 1 tonne of hard coral for the aquarium trade is worth US$7,000 but as construction material only US$60. Live rock exported for the aquarium trade is worth US$2 per kilo but less than US$0.2 per kilo as construction material (Palau).
  • Thousands of poor people depend on the trade for a living.

Links

  • From Ocean to Aquarium: The global trade in marine ornamental species on the Reef Check website: UNEP and WCMC 2003 paper detailing the trade, impact and conservation efforts (PDF)
  • Bad Fish List on aquariumpros.com website: a list of fishes that should not be kept in captivity with the tagline “If It Won’t Live in Aquaria, Leave It in the Ocean!”
